terms.definition: The whole group whose characteristics a study is meant to describe, as distinct from the smaller group that the study actually enrolled.

The list or route through which a study can actually reach people — a resident register, an appointment list, the room a recruitment poster hangs in. A sample can at best represent its frame, and a frame can fall short of the target population.

Drawing a sample from the sampling frame by a chance mechanism that gives every member a known chance of selection; the simplest version is plain random selection. It supports generalizing from the sample toward the frame's population. Random selection decides who enters a study and is distinct from random allocation, which decides which arm an already-entered participant joins.

A group selected because its members were easy to reach or willing to volunteer, rather than by a chance mechanism giving every member of the sampling frame a known chance of selection. In this lesson it is the standard example of a route that narrows or misdirects who a result is about.
One of the groups being compared in a trial. A trial comparing a new routine against sitting quietly has two arms.
The degree to which a study's own comparison supports the conclusion drawn about the people or items that were in it.
The degree to which a study's conclusion carries over to people, settings, and conditions outside the study. Also called applicability or generalizability.

Deciding each participant's condition by an unpredictable chance mechanism rather than by any characteristic of the participant, so that the compared groups are comparable in expectation on measured and unmeasured factors alike.

A property of the assignment mechanism, not of the groups that actually resulted: averaged over every way the chance mechanism could have come out, the groups match. Any single study can still be imbalanced.

Any arrangement ensuring that a participant's entry into the study is irrevocably committed before their upcoming assignment can be known by anyone in a position to act on it — commitment first, knowability second — so that who enters which group cannot be steered.

Withholding knowledge of the assigned condition from people involved in a study, from the moment of allocation until the study ends. Applies separately to participants, providers, data collectors, outcome assessors, and analysts.

Distortion produced by how people or items came to be in the groups being compared, rather than by anything done to them afterwards. Steering who enters which arm is the form of it that allocation concealment prevents.

Distortion arising when participants or those delivering an intervention behave differently because they know the assigned condition — for example through changed effort, adherence, treatment sought elsewhere, or withdrawal.
Distortion arising when an outcome is measured, assessed, or adjudicated differently because the assessor knows the assigned condition. Also called ascertainment bias.

An outcome whose value depends on a person's rating, interpretation, or judgement — such as a self-reported score or a clinician's assessment — as opposed to one recorded by an instrument or automatic process. Subjective outcomes are the most exposed to detection bias.
The person or process that measures, scores, or adjudicates the study outcome. Blinding this role is the usual protection when participants themselves cannot be blinded.
